participants.extracted: Participants were 64 adult trauma inpatients admitted to a Mid-western Level-1 trauma unit, aged 18–56 years (mean 30.6 ± 10.7), including 34% female and 84% Caucasian, 14% African American, and 2% Native American. All had experienced a qualifying traumatic event, were non-amnestic, had a Glasgow Coma Scale score of at least 14, and were deemed at high risk for PTSD based on a Peritraumatic Dissociative Experiences Questionnaire (PDEQ) score of 27 or higher. Exclusion criteria included trauma more than 12 hours before first dose could be given, allergy or contraindication to cortisol, pregnancy or breastfeeding, ongoing-trauma exposure such as domestic violence, injuries requiring delayed operative procedures, recent corticosteroid use, or injuries requiring steroid treatment. Participants were randomized to hydrocortisone (n = 31) or placebo (n = 33).

interventions.extracted: Participants were randomized in double-blind fashion to receive either oral hydrocortisone or matching placebo. The active intervention was hydrocortisone 20 mg by mouth twice daily for 10 days, followed by a 6-day taper in which the dose was halved every 2 days to avoid adrenal suppression. The first dose was administered within 12 hours of hospital admission after the traumatic event, and adherence was monitored by diary self-reports and pill counts.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) incidence and PTSD symptom severity using the Clinician-Administered PTSD Scale (CAPS) at 1 month and 3 months post-injury. It also planned to measure depressive symptoms using the Center for Epidemiological Studies–Depression Scale (CES-D) at 1 month and 3 months post-injury, and health-related quality of life using the RAND SF-36, focusing on the general health subscale, at 1 month and 3 months post-injury. In addition, prior mental health treatment was assessed at baseline by self-report (“ever received help from a mental health professional”) as a potential moderator, and peritraumatic dissociation and peritraumatic distress were assessed in-hospital using the Peritraumatic Dissociative Experiences Questionnaire (PDEQ) and the Peritraumatic Distress Inventory (PDI), respectively, for screening and baseline characterization.
